17.text: Threading: the prefill and decode workers run in parallel, communicating through a queue — simulating separate GPU workers.

19.text: Thread-safe FIFO queue connecting the prefill worker to the decode worker.

26.text: Fix seeds for reproducible workload generation.

47.text: Small model config — enough to demonstrate the serving architecture.

64.text: Self-attention WITH KV cache support — the distinction between prefill and decode lives here.

72.text: Accepts an optional KV cache: None for prefill (process all tokens), populated for decode (only new token's K/V are computed).

73.text: T_new is the full prompt length during prefill, but just 1 during decode.

74.text: Project queries for the new tokens only.

77.text: If we have cached K/V from previous steps, concatenate the new K/V onto them.

78.text: Concatenate new keys with cached keys — the cache grows by one token each decode step.

80.text: Total sequence length including cached history.

81.text: Attention scores: new queries attend to ALL keys (cached + new).

82.text: Causal mask adapted for the asymmetric case: T_new query rows vs T_total key columns. During decode, this is just 1 row attending to the full history.

83.text: Mask and softmax in one line.

84.text: Weighted values, reshape to (B, T_new, C).

85.text: Return output AND the updated cache — the caller decides whether to keep it.

98.text: Transformer block that threads KV caches through.

106.text: Block forward: attention with cache, MLP, return updated cache.

110.text: Return both output and the new cache.

113.text: Full GPT with explicit prefill and decode methods — the foundation for disaggregated serving.

128.text: Forward pass with KV cache support. start_pos offsets the position embeddings — during decode, positions continue from where prefill left off.

130.text: Position embeddings offset by start_pos — during decode step 5, this produces position embedding for position 5, not 0.

131.text: Collect updated caches from each layer.

133.text: Pass the previous cache (if any) to each layer.

135.text: Return logits and all updated caches.

137.text: Prefill: process the entire prompt in one parallel forward pass. This is COMPUTE-BOUND — lots of tokens, high parallelism, saturates GPU ALUs.

138.text: Just a forward pass with no prior cache — all tokens processed together.

140.text: Decode: process ONE new token using the cached KV history. This is MEMORY-BOUND — reading all cached K/V values but computing very little.

141.text: Single token forward pass with position offset.

149.text: Adam optimizer — same settings as all labs.

158.text: Forward pass — ignoring caches during training.

168.text: Switch to eval mode for inference.

169.text: Disable gradient tracking globally — everything from here is inference.

177.text: Timing utility: warms up with 5 runs, then times 50 runs for stable measurement.

178.text: Warmup runs — let caches and JIT settle.

186.text: Measure the compute profile difference between prefill and decode at various prompt lengths.

187.text: Create a random prompt of length plen.

188.text: Decode up to 5 tokens after the prompt.

189.text: Time the prefill phase: ALL prompt tokens processed in one pass.

190.text: Get the initial KV caches from prefill.

192.text: Time the decode phase: generate tokens one at a time.

194.text: Each decode step processes one token.

195.text: One token forward pass with KV cache.

200.text: Prefill FLOPs scale as O(T^2) — quadratic in prompt length. Decode FLOPs scale linearly per step but add up.

207.text: Test with increasing prompt lengths to show how prefill scales quadratically while decode stays relatively flat.

209.text: Print timing and FLOPs for this prompt length.

223.text: Simulated prefill cost: 0.5ms per prompt token. On real GPUs, this would be actual compute time.

224.text: Simulated decode cost: 0.3ms per step. Cheaper per-step than prefill because it's just one token.

228.text: A serving request: prompt, timing metadata, and generated tokens.

231.text: When this request arrives in the system.

233.text: Time-to-first-token: the key latency metric for user experience.

238.text: Create a realistic workload: mix of short prompts (fast prefill) and long prompts (slow prefill). The long prompts are the problem — they block everything in colocated serving.

242.text: 30% chance of a long prompt (8-12 tokens) — these are the "heavy" requests that cause head-of-line blocking.

244.text: Stagger arrival times by 0.3ms — simulating real request arrivals.

248.text: Simulate a prefill operation: run the actual model AND sleep to simulate GPU time.

249.text: Real forward pass to get logits and KV caches.

250.text: Sleep proportional to prompt length — longer prompts take longer to prefill.

254.text: Simulate a decode step: real model computation + simulated GPU time.

255.text: Real single-token forward pass with KV cache.

260.text: Sample one token from the model's output distribution.

261.text: Apply temperature and sample — the actual token generation.

265.text: Colocated serving: ONE worker does both prefill AND decode. Processes requests FIFO — a long prefill blocks ALL subsequent requests.

268.text: Wait until the request's arrival time.

269.text: Sleep if the request hasn't arrived yet.

271.text: Prefill the prompt — if this is a LONG prompt, all subsequent requests wait.

272.text: Record time-to-first-token — this is terrible for requests stuck behind long prefills.

273.text: Sample the first token and start decoding.

274.text: Decode loop: generate tokens one at a time.

275.text: Stop on BOS (end token) or context limit.

278.text: One decode step with KV cache.

279.text: Sample next token and advance position.

286.text: Disaggregated serving: SEPARATE prefill and decode workers connected by a queue. Prefill doesn't block decode. The key insight: while one worker prefills a long prompt, the other worker continues decoding in-flight requests.

287.text: Thread-safe queue connects prefill worker to decode worker.

288.text: Results collection with lock for thread safety.

289.text: Signal when prefill is done; wall-clock baseline.

291.text: Prefill worker: processes prompts and hands off to decode worker.

293.text: Wait for each request's arrival time.

296.text: Prefill the prompt — runs independently of any ongoing decoding.

297.text: Record time-to-first-token — now only depends on THIS request's prefill, not previous requests.

299.text: Hand off the prefilled state (KV caches, first token, position) to the decode worker.

300.text: Signal that all prefills are done.

302.text: Decode worker: picks up prefilled requests and generates tokens. Can interleave decoding of multiple requests.

304.text: Keep running until prefill is done and all decodes finish.

305.text: Check the queue for newly prefilled requests.

306.text: Drain the queue — grab all available prefilled requests.

307.text: Unpack the handoff: request, KV caches, first sampled token, position.

308.text: Add to active set with an empty generated tokens list.

309.text: If nothing active and prefill not done, briefly wait.

310.text: Check if prefill worker has finished.

314.text: Process one decode step for each active request.

317.text: Request finished: record timing and results.

321.text: Continue generating: append token, decode one step, sample next.

322.text: One decode step with KV cache.

327.text: Start both workers — they run in parallel.

331.text: Sort results by request ID for clean comparison.

338.text: Label long vs short prompts — long ones are the troublemakers.

346.text: Same seed ensures same model outputs.

357.text: Compare each request between the two strategies.

359.text: TTFT speedup: how much faster was disaggregated for this request?

360.text: Decode generated tokens back to characters.

371.text: Average TTFT across all requests — the headline metric.

373.text: Print the TTFT comparison. Disaggregated should show significantly lower average TTFT, especially for requests that arrived after long prompts.

374.text: Total wall-clock time for all requests to complete.
